title: 75.	Find number of subarrays with gcd = k.
description: 75.	Find number of subarrays with gcd = k.

examples.explanation: Array: [4,2,6,8], k=2. All subarrays and their GCDs: [4] GCD=4 ≠2, [4,2] GCD=gcd(4,2)=2 ✓, [4,2,6] GCD=gcd(gcd(4,2),6)=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓, [4,2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(4,2),6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓, [2] GCD=2 ✓, [2,6] GCD=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓, [2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(2,6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓, [6] GCD=6 ≠2, [6,8] GCD=gcd(6,8)=2 ✓, [8] GCD=8 ≠2. Total subarrays with GCD=2: [4,2], [4,2,6], [4,2,6,8], [2], [2,6], [2,6,8], [6,8] = 7 subarrays. Wait, recount: [4,2] ✓, [4,2,6] ✓, [4,2,6,8] ✓, [2] ✓, [2,6] ✓, [2,6,8] ✓, [6,8] ✓ = 7. But my initial count was 6, let's recalculate systematically: Start i=0: j=0: subarray [4] GCD=4 ≠2; j=1: [4,2] GCD=2 ✓ count=1; j=2: [4,2,6] GCD=gcd(gcd(4,2),6)=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓ count=2; j=3: [4,2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(4,2),6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓ count=3. i=1: j=1: [2] GCD=2 ✓ count=4; j=2: [2,6] GCD=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓ count=5; j=3: [2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(2,6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓ count=6. i=2: j=2: [6] GCD=6 ≠2; j=3: [6,8] GCD=gcd(6,8)=2 ✓ count=7. i=3: j=3: [8] GCD=8 ≠2. Total count=7. So output should be 7, not 6. Correcting: output=7.

Array: [3,6,9,12], k=3. All subarrays: i=0: j=0: [3] GCD=3 ✓ count=1; j=1: [3,6] GCD=gcd(3,6)=3 ✓ count=2; j=2: [3,6,9] GCD=gcd(gcd(3,6),9)=gcd(3,9)=3 ✓ count=3; j=3: [3,6,9,12] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(3,6),9),12)=gcd(3,12)=3 ✓ count=4. i=1: j=1: [6] GCD=6 ≠3; j=2: [6,9] GCD=gcd(6,9)=3 ✓ count=5; j=3: [6,9,12] GCD=gcd(gcd(6,9),12)=gcd(3,12)=3 ✓ count=6. i=2: j=2: [9] GCD=9 ≠3; j=3: [9,12] GCD=gcd(9,12)=3 ✓ count=7. i=3: j=3: [12] GCD=12 ≠3. Total count=7. Wait, that's 7, but expected output is 10. Let's list all subarrays: [3]✓, [3,6]✓, [3,6,9]✓, [3,6,9,12]✓, [6]✗, [6,9]✓, [6,9,12]✓, [9]✗, [9,12]✓, [12]✗. That's 6 subarrays? No: [3]✓, [3,6]✓, [3,6,9]✓, [3,6,9,12]✓ =4; [6]✗, [6,9]✓, [6,9,12]✓ =2 more total 6; [9]✗, [9,12]✓ =1 more total 7; [12]✗ total 7. So output should be 7. Perhaps the expected output is wrong? Let's double-check: The problem might count subarrays where GCD is exactly k, not divisible by k. Here all GCDs are multiples of 3: [3]=3, [3,6]=3, [3,6,9]=3, [3,6,9,12]=3, [6]=6, [6,9]=3, [6,9,12]=3, [9]=9, [9,12]=3, [12]=12. So subarrays with GCD=3 are: [3], [3,6], [3,6,9], [3,6,9,12], [6,9], [6,9,12], [9,12] =7. So output=7. I'll correct expected output to 7.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all possible subarrays by using two nested loops for start and end indices, and compute the GCD for each subarray in a third loop. Count subarrays where GCD equals k. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with GCD = k using Brute Force O(n³)
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all subarrays where GCD equals k using triple nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach for counting subarrays with GCD equal to k. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible contiguous subarray in the array, compute its GCD, and check if it equals k. Since we're using brute force, we don't optimize any part of this process. We use three nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index, the middle loop fixes the ending index, and the inner loop computes the GCD of the current subarray. For each subarray, we compute the GCD by iterating through all elements between start and end indices, applying the Euclidean algorithm repeatedly. If the computed GCD equals k, we increment our count. The time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) subarrays and computing GCD for each takes O(n) in the worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large n but serves as a baseline solution that's easy to understand and implement. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the case where k=0 properly (GCD of any subarray with 0 is undefined, but the problem likely assumes positive integers), 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, 3) Not resetting the GCD calculation for each new subarray, 4) Confusing subarrays with subsequences (subarrays must be contiguous). The connection to L1 is that this level uses the GCD computation from L1 as a subroutine within the triple nested loop structure.

examples.trace: Array: [4,2,6,8], k=2. All subarrays and their GCDs: [4] GCD=4 ≠2, [4,2] GCD=gcd(4,2)=2 ✓, [4,2,6] GCD=gcd(gcd(4,2),6)=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓, [4,2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(4,2),6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓, [2] GCD=2 ✓, [2,6] GCD=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓, [2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(2,6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓, [6] GCD=6 ≠2, [6,8] GCD=gcd(6,8)=2 ✓, [8] GCD=8 ≠2. Total subarrays with GCD=2: [4,2], [4,2,6], [4,2,6,8], [2], [2,6], [2,6,8], [6,8] = 7 subarrays. Wait, recount: [4,2] ✓, [4,2,6] ✓, [4,2,6,8] ✓, [2] ✓, [2,6] ✓, [2,6,8] ✓, [6,8] ✓ = 7. But my initial count was 6, let's recalculate systematically: Start i=0: j=0: subarray [4] GCD=4 ≠2; j=1: [4,2] GCD=2 ✓ count=1; j=2: [4,2,6] GCD=gcd(gcd(4,2),6)=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓ count=2; j=3: [4,2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(4,2),6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓ count=3. i=1: j=1: [2] GCD=2 ✓ count=4; j=2: [2,6] GCD=gcd(2,6)=2 ✓ count=5; j=3: [2,6,8] GCD=gcd(gcd(2,6),8)=gcd(2,8)=2 ✓ count=6. i=2: j=2: [6] GCD=6 ≠2; j=3: [6,8] GCD=gcd(6,8)=2 ✓ count=7. i=3: j=3: [8] GCD=8 ≠2. Total count=7. So output should be 7, not 6. Correcting: output=7.

Array: [3,6,9,12], k=3. All subarrays: i=0: j=0: [3] GCD=3 ✓ count=1; j=1: [3,6] GCD=gcd(3,6)=3 ✓ count=2; j=2: [3,6,9] GCD=gcd(gcd(3,6),9)=gcd(3,9)=3 ✓ count=3; j=3: [3,6,9,12] GCD=gcd(gcd(gcd(3,6),9),12)=gcd(3,12)=3 ✓ count=4. i=1: j=1: [6] GCD=6 ≠3; j=2: [6,9] GCD=gcd(6,9)=3 ✓ count=5; j=3: [6,9,12] GCD=gcd(gcd(6,9),12)=gcd(3,12)=3 ✓ count=6. i=2: j=2: [9] GCD=9 ≠3; j=3: [9,12] GCD=gcd(9,12)=3 ✓ count=7. i=3: j=3: [12] GCD=12 ≠3. Total count=7. Wait, that's 7, but expected output is 10. Let's list all subarrays: [3]✓, [3,6]✓, [3,6,9]✓, [3,6,9,12]✓, [6]✗, [6,9]✓, [6,9,12]✓, [9]✗, [9,12]✓, [12]✗. That's 6 subarrays? No: [3]✓, [3,6]✓, [3,6,9]✓, [3,6,9,12]✓ =4; [6]✗, [6,9]✓, [6,9,12]✓ =2 more total 6; [9]✗, [9,12]✓ =1 more total 7; [12]✗ total 7. So output should be 7. Perhaps the expected output is wrong? Let's double-check: The problem might count subarrays where GCD is exactly k, not divisible by k. Here all GCDs are multiples of 3: [3]=3, [3,6]=3, [3,6,9]=3, [3,6,9,12]=3, [6]=6, [6,9]=3, [6,9,12]=3, [9]=9, [9,12]=3, [12]=12. So subarrays with GCD=3 are: [3], [3,6], [3,6,9], [3,6,9,12], [6,9], [6,9,12], [9,12] =7. So output=7. I'll correct expected output to 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute GCD of a Subarray using Iterative Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Calculate the greatest common divisor of all elements in a given subarray range.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the GCD of all elements in a contiguous subarray defined by start and end indices. The intuition is that GCD is associative: gcd(a,b,c) = gcd(gcd(a,b),c). Therefore, we can compute the GCD incrementally by iterating through the elements from start to end, updating the current GCD with each new element. We use the Euclidean algorithm to compute GCD of two numbers: repeatedly apply gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) until b becomes 0. For a subarray, we initialize result as the first element, then for each subsequent element, we update result = gcd(result, current_element). The time complexity for a subarray of length m is O(m * log(min(a,b))) where the log factor comes from the Euclidean algorithm, but in worst-case analysis for brute force, we simplify to O(m). Space complexity is O(1). This is the core computation needed in the brute force approach. Common mistakes: 1) Not handling empty subarrays (but in our problem, subarrays have at least one element), 2) Forgetting to reset the GCD when starting a new subarray, 3) Incorrect implementation of Euclidean algorithm (e.g., not using modulo properly), 4) Not considering that GCD of a single element is the element itself. The connection to L0 is that this function is called inside the innermost loop for each subarray to determine if its GCD equals k.

examples.trace: Compute GCD of subarray [12,18,24]. Initialize result = arr[0] = 12. Process element 18: gcd(12,18). Euclidean: 18 mod 12 = 6, then gcd(12,6): 12 mod 6 = 0, so gcd=6. Update result = 6. Process element 24: gcd(6,24). Euclidean: 24 mod 6 = 0, so gcd=6. Final result = 6.

Subarray from index 1 to 3: [14,21,28]. Initialize result = arr[1] = 14. Process element 21: gcd(14,21). Euclidean: 21 mod 14 = 7, then gcd(14,7): 14 mod 7 = 0, so gcd=7. Update result = 7. Process element 28: gcd(7,28). Euclidean: 28 mod 7 = 0, so gcd=7. Final result = 7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Subarrays Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays by varying start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on generating all contiguous subarrays of an array, which is the structural core of the brute force approach. The intuition is that any subarray can be uniquely identified by its starting index i and ending index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n. By using two nested loops, we can systematically enumerate all such pairs. The outer loop iterates over all possible starting indices from 0 to n-1. For each start i, the inner loop iterates over all possible ending indices from i to n-1. This ensures we cover every contiguous subarray exactly once. The number of subarrays is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). This is a fundamental pattern in array problems that require examining all subarrays. Time complexity for this generation alone is O(n²), but in the full brute force solution, we add an inner loop for GCD computation making it O(n³). Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong loop boundaries (e.g., j starting from 0 instead of i), 2) Including non-contiguous sequences, 3) Not understanding that subarrays are defined by contiguous indices. The connection to L0 is that this loop structure forms the outer two loops of the triple nested loop, and for each (i,j) pair, we then compute the GCD of the subarray arr[i..j].

examples.trace: n=3. Outer loop i from 0 to 2: i=0: inner loop j from 0 to 2: j=0: subarray [1]; j=1: [1,2]; j=2: [1,2,3]. i=1: j from 1 to 2: j=1: [2]; j=2: [2,3]. i=2: j from 2 to 2: j=2: [3]. Total 6 subarrays.

ladders.title: Building Block — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental algorithm for computing GCD, which is essential for our problem. The intuition is based on the mathematical property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of division, we reduce the numbers until one becomes zero, at which point the other number is the GCD. The algorithm works because the GCD of two numbers doesn't change if we subtract the smaller from the larger, and modulo operation is a efficient way to do this subtraction repeatedly. Step-by-step: 1) While b is not 0, 2) Set temp = b, 3) Update b = a mod b, 4) Set a = temp, 5) When b becomes 0, return a. Alternatively, we can use recursion: gcd(a,b) = b if a==0 else gcd(b%a, a). Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) as the numbers reduce quickly. Space complexity is O(1) for iterative version, O(log n) for recursive due to call stack. This is a building block because in L1 we extend this to compute GCD of multiple numbers by applying it sequentially. Common mistakes: 1) Not handling negative numbers (but in our problem, inputs are positive), 2) Infinite loop if modulo operation is implemented incorrectly, 3) Confusing GCD with LCM, 4) Not considering that gcd(0,a) = a. The connection to L1 is that this two-number GCD function is called repeatedly to compute the GCD of an entire subarray.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(48,18). Iteration 1: b=18 ≠0, temp=18, b=48 mod 18=12, a=18. Iteration 2: b=12 ≠0, temp=12, b=18 mod 12=6, a=12. Iteration 3: b=6 ≠0, temp=6, b=12 mod 6=0, a=6. Now b=0, so gcd=6.
Compute gcd(17,13). Iteration 1: b=13 ≠0, temp=13, b=17 mod 13=4, a=13. Iteration 2: b=4 ≠0, temp=4, b=13 mod 4=1, a=4. Iteration 3: b=1 ≠0, temp=1, b=4 mod 1=0, a=1. b=0, so gcd=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the modulo operation, which is crucial for the Euclidean algorithm. The intuition is that when we divide a by b, we get a quotient and a remainder r such that a = b*q + r where 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo operation a % b gives this remainder r. For example, 17 % 5 = 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. In programming, % is the modulo operator. It's a basic arithmetic operation like addition or multiplication. Key properties: 1) a % b is always between 0 and b-1, 2) If a < b, then a % b = a, 3) a % 1 = 0 for any a, 4) a % a = 0. In the Euclidean algorithm, we use modulo to reduce numbers efficiently: instead of repeatedly subtracting b from a, we compute a % b which gives the same result but faster. Time complexity of modulo is typically O(1) in hardware, but for large numbers it depends on implementation. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes: 1) Confusing modulo with integer division, 2) Not understanding negative modulo results (different languages handle this differently; in Python, -17 % 5 = 3, but in our problem we deal with positive numbers), 3) Division by zero error when b=0. The connection to L3 is that modulo is the core operation in the Euclidean algorithm for computing GCD.

examples.trace: Compute 17 % 5. 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3 and remainder 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. So remainder = 2.
Compute 8 % 3. 8 divided by 3: 3*2=6, remainder 8-6=2. So 8 % 3 = 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subarrays and Contiguous Sequences
ladders.desc: Identify what constitutes a subarray in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept needed for the problem: understanding what a subarray is. A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements from an array. Contiguous means the elements are consecutive in the original array, with no gaps. For example, in array [1,2,3,4], [2,3] is a subarray, but [1,3] is not because it skips element 2. Subarrays are defined by their starting index and ending index. The number of subarrays in an array of length n is n*(n+1)/2. This formula comes from: for each starting index i (0 to n-1), there are n-i possible ending indices j (i to n-1). Summing over i gives total subarrays. Intuition: think of all possible slices of the array. This is different from subsequences, which can be non-contiguous. In programming, we often work with subarrays using loops that vary start and end indices. Time complexity to list all subarrays is O(n²) because there are O(n²) of them. Space complexity depends on what we do with them. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing subarrays with subsets or subsequences, 2) Thinking subarrays must have at least 2 elements (they can have 1 element), 3) Not realizing that the entire array is also a subarray. The connection to L2 is that this concept is why we use nested loops to generate all (i,j) pairs.

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Starting index i=0: ending indices j=0 gives [5], j=1 gives [5,10], j=2 gives [5,10,15]. i=1: j=1 gives [10], j=2 gives [10,15]. i=2: j=2 gives [15]. Total 6 subarrays.
Length n=2. i=0: j=0 [2], j=1 [2,4]. i=1: j=1 [4]. Total 3 subarrays.

approaches.name: Optimized Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all subarrays by fixing a start index and extending the end index, maintaining a running GCD as you extend. Count subarrays where the running GCD equals k. This reduces time by avoiding recomputation of GCD for overlapping subarrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with GCD = k using Optimized Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all subarrays where GCD equals k using O(n²) optimized brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all contiguous subarrays of an array where the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all elements equals a given value k. The optimized brute force approach improves upon naive O(n³) by fixing a start index and extending the end index while maintaining a running GCD. As we extend the subarray, we compute GCD(current_running_gcd, new_element) instead of recomputing GCD from scratch for each subarray. This leverages the property that GCD is associative: GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b),c). The algorithm iterates through all possible start indices (0 to n-1). For each start, initialize running_gcd = arr[start]. If running_gcd == k, increment count. Then extend end index from start+1 to n-1: update running_gcd = gcd(running_gcd, arr[end]), and if running_gcd == k, increment count. Stop extending if running_gcd < k since GCD can only decrease or stay same as we add elements. Time complexity is O(n² * logM) where M is max array value (GCD computation cost). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the gcd() helper function from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include not stopping early when running_gcd < k (wasted computation), incorrect GCD computation for single elements, or misunderstanding that subarrays must be contiguous. Real code pattern: def count_subarrays_gcd_k(arr, k): n=len(arr); count=0; for i in range(n): curr_gcd=arr[i]; if curr_gcd==k: count+=1; for j in range(i+1,n): curr_gcd=gcd(curr_gcd,arr[j]); if curr_gcd==k: count+=1; elif curr_gcd<k: break; return count.

examples.trace: Start i=0: arr[0]=3, curr_gcd=3 ==3 → count=1. j=1: curr_gcd=gcd(3,6)=3 → count=2. j=2: curr_gcd=gcd(3,9)=3 → count=3. j=3: curr_gcd=gcd(3,12)=3 → count=4. i=1: arr[1]=6, curr_gcd=6 ≠3. j=2: curr_gcd=gcd(6,9)=3 → count=5. j=3: curr_gcd=gcd(3,12)=3 → count=6. i=2: arr[2]=9, curr_gcd=9 ≠3. j=3: curr_gcd=gcd(9,12)=3 → count=7 (but wait, check: arr[2]=9≠3, so no count for single element). Actually recount: i=0: [3],[3,6],[3,6,9],[3,6,9,12] → 4. i=1: [6,9],[6,9,12] → 2. i=2: [9,12] → 1. Total 7? Let's compute carefully: i=0: start at 3: subarray [3] GCD=3 count1, [3,6] GCD=3 count2, [3,6,9] GCD=3 count3, [3,6,9,12] GCD=3 count4. i=1: start at 6: [6] GCD=6≠3 no count, [6,9] GCD=3 count5, [6,9,12] GCD=3 count6. i=2: start at 9: [9] GCD=9≠3 no count, [9,12] GCD=3 count7. i=3: start at 12: [12] GCD=12≠3 no count. Total 7. But expected output is 6. Let's verify: arr=[3,6,9,12], k=3. All elements divisible by 3. Subarrays: [3],[6],[9],[12],[3,6],[3,6,9],[3,6,9,12],[6,9],[6,9,12],[9,12]. GCDs: 3,6,9,12,3,3,3,3,3,3. Those with GCD=3: [3],[3,6],[3,6,9],[3,6,9,12],[6,9],[6,9,12],[9,12] → 7. So expected should be 7. I'll correct output to 7. Trace: i=0: curr_gcd=3==3 count=1; extend to 6: gcd(3,6)=3 count=2; to 9: gcd(3,9)=3 count=3; to 12: gcd(3,12)=3 count=4. i=1: curr_gcd=6≠3; extend to 9: gcd(6,9)=3 count=5; to 12: gcd(3,12)=3 count=6. i=2: curr_gcd=9≠3; extend to 12: gcd(9,12)=3 count=7. i=3: curr_gcd=12≠3. Total 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — GCD Computation using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the gcd() function using Euclidean algorithm to compute greatest common divisor of two numbers.
ladders.explanation: The gcd (greatest common divisor) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This recursive property allows efficient computation by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of division until one number becomes 0, at which point the other number is the gcd. Intuition: If d divides both a and b, then it also divides a-b and a mod b. By reducing the problem size each step, we quickly converge. Step-by-step: function gcd(a,b): while b ≠ 0: temp = b; b = a % b; a = temp; return a. For negative numbers, take absolute values. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) as each step reduces numbers significantly. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to compute running GCD. Common mistakes include not handling zeros (gcd(a,0)=|a|), infinite loops with negative numbers, or using slow naive method. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): a,b=abs(a),abs(b); while b: a,b=b,a%b; return a.

examples.trace: Step1: a=48, b=18. b≠0, compute a%b=48%18=12. Update: a=18, b=12. Step2: b≠0, compute 18%12=6. Update: a=12, b=6. Step3: b≠0, compute 12%6=0. Update: a=6, b=0. Step4: b=0, return a=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Running GCD While Extending Subarrays
ladders.desc: Technique of fixing start index and extending end while updating GCD incrementally to avoid recomputation.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that when computing GCD of a subarray [i..j], we can use the previously computed GCD of [i..j-1] and combine it with arr[j] using gcd() function, rather than recomputing GCD from scratch for each subarray. This works because GCD is associative: gcd(a,b,c) = gcd(gcd(a,b), c). By fixing start index i and iteratively extending j from i to n-1, we maintain a running GCD variable that starts as arr[i] and gets updated as gcd(running_gcd, arr[j]) for each new j. This reduces time from O(n³) to O(n²) since each subarray's GCD is computed in O(1) amortized time (plus GCD computation cost). Intuition: Overlapping subarrays share prefixes; we reuse computations. Step-by-step: for i in 0..n-1: curr = arr[i]; process subarray [i,i]; for j in i+1..n-1: curr = gcd(curr, arr[j]); process subarray [i,j]. Stop if curr < k because GCD can only decrease. Time per start index is O(n * logM). Space O(1). Connection to L0: This is the main loop structure of the full solution. Connection to L1: Uses gcd() function. Common mistakes: Not stopping early when curr < k, incorrectly initializing curr, or misunderstanding that subarrays are contiguous. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): curr=arr[i]; # process; for j in range(i+1,n): curr=gcd(curr,arr[j]); # process; if curr<k: break.

examples.output: Running GCDs for subarrays starting at index 0
examples.trace: Start i=0: curr=arr[0]=6. Subarray [0,0] GCD=6. Extend j=1: curr=gcd(6,9)=3 → subarray [0,1] GCD=3. Extend j=2: curr=gcd(3,12)=3 → subarray [0,2] GCD=3. So running GCDs: 6,3,3.

Start i=1: curr=arr[1]=8. Since 8>4, continue. Subarray [1,1] GCD=8. Extend j=2: curr=gcd(8,12)=4 → subarray [1,2] GCD=4. If k=4, we count when curr==4. If we had k=2, after curr becomes 4 which is >2, we continue; but if curr became 1 which is <2, we would stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Generate all contiguous subarrays of an array using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: A contiguous subarray is a sequence of consecutive elements from the original array. For array of length n, there are n*(n+1)/2 such subarrays. To iterate over all of them, we use two nested loops: outer loop fixes start index i from 0 to n-1, inner loop fixes end index j from i to n-1. This generates subarrays [i..j] in order: first all starting at 0, then all starting at 1, etc. Intuition: By controlling start and end pointers, we cover all possible contiguous segments. Step-by-step: for i in 0..n-1: for j in i..n-1: process subarray arr[i..j]. Time O(n²) to generate all subarrays. Space O(1) if not storing them. Connection to L2: This iteration structure is used in the running GCD technique where we process each subarray as we generate it. Common mistakes: Using wrong bounds (e.g., j from 0), generating non-contiguous subsequences, or misunderstanding total count. Real code pattern: n=len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # subarray arr[i:j+1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute remainder of division using modulo operator %.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation a % b returns the remainder when a is divided by b. For integers a and b (b ≠ 0), a % b = a - b * floor(a/b). In programming, % handles positive and negative numbers based on language; in Python, result has same sign as divisor. Intuition: It's useful for reducing numbers in algorithms like Euclidean GCD. Step-by-step: To compute a % b, divide a by b, take integer quotient q = a // b, remainder r = a - b*q. Time O(1). Space O(1). Connection to L1: Used in gcd() implementation. Common mistakes: Division by zero, misunderstanding negative remainders, or confusing with integer division. Real code pattern: remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3, remainder 17 - 5*3 = 2.
10 / 3 = 3 quotient, remainder 10 - 3*3 = 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subarrays and Contiguity
ladders.desc: Define what a subarray is and distinguish from subsequence.
ladders.explanation: A subarray is a contiguous portion of an array. It must consist of consecutive elements from the original array, maintaining their order. For example, from array [A,B,C,D], valid subarrays include [A], [A,B], [B,C,D], but not [A,C] because that skips B (non-contiguous). Contrast with subsequence which can skip elements but maintain order. Intuition: Think of subarray as a slice or segment of the array. Step-by-step: Given array of length n, a subarray is defined by start index i and end index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n, and includes all elements from i to j inclusive. Total number of subarrays = n*(n+1)/2. Time to list them O(n²). Space O(1) per subarray if generated on fly. Connection to L3: This concept is prerequisite for iterating over subarrays. Common mistakes: Confusing with subsets (which can be any combination) or subsequences, or including non-contiguous elements. Real code pattern: subarray = arr[i:j+1] in Python.
